docs.title: rxn-ca
docs.text: A lattice model for simulating solid state reactions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: rxn-ca is a Python library for predicting the outcome of solid-state synthesis reactions using a cellular automaton approach. It leverages thermodynamic data from the Materials Project to enumerate possible reactions and simulate phase evolution during synthesis.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Reaction Enumeration: Automatically enumerate possible reactions between precursor phases using thermodynamic data
  • Temperature-Dependent Kinetics: Compute reaction energies at any temperature using the SISSO descriptor
  • Cellular Automaton Simulation: Model spatial evolution of phases on a lattice grid
  • Heating Profile Support: Define complex heating schedules with temperature sweeps and holds
  • HPC Integration: Run parallel simulations using Jobflow for high-throughput studies
  • Analysis Tools: Visualize phase evolution and extract quantitative metrics

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from rxn_ca.core.recipe import ReactionRecipe
from rxn_ca.core.heating import HeatingSchedule, HeatingStep

# Define reactants (mole ratios)
reactants = {"MgO": 1, "Al2O3": 1}

# Create heating schedule
heating_schedule = HeatingSchedule.build(
    HeatingStep.sweep(500, 1600, stage_length=1, temp_step_size=50),
    HeatingStep.hold(1600, stage_length=20)
)

# Create recipe
recipe = ReactionRecipe(
    reactant_amounts=reactants,
    heating_schedule=heating_schedule
)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started - Installation and setup
  • Basic Usage - Complete walkthrough of running a simulation
  • Using Jobflow - Running parallel simulations on HPC

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: rxn-ca provides several command-line tools for running simulations and preparing inputs.

docs.title: react
docs.text:
Run a cellular automaton reaction simulation.

react <recipe_file> -l <library_file> -o <output_file> [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description recipe_file Path to the reaction recipe JSON file

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -l, --library Path to the reaction library JSON file -o, --output Output file path --compress Compress output (recommended)

docs.title: Example
docs.text: react spinel_recipe.json -l reaction_library.json -o output.json --compress

docs.title: enumerate
docs.text:
Enumerate reactions for a chemical system.

enumerate <chem_sys> [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description chem_sys Chemical system (e.g., "Mg-Al-O")

docs.title: build-library
docs.text:
Build a reaction library from enumerated reactions.

build-library [options]

docs.title: suggest-precursors
docs.text:
Generate suggested precursor sets for a target phase.

suggest-precursors <target> [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description target Target phase formula (e.g., "BaTiO3")

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
suggest-precursors BaTiO3

This generates practical precursor suggestions including oxides, carbonates, hydroxides, and nitrates based on the target composition.

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
Clone the repository and install in development mode:

git clone https://github.com/mcgalcode/rxn-ca.git
cd rxn-ca
pip install -e .

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text:
For optimization features:

pip install -e ".[optimization]"

For workflow integration with Jobflow:

pip install -e ".[workflow]"

For visualization tools:

pip install -e ".[vis]"

docs.title: Materials Project API Key
docs.text:
rxn-ca retrieves thermodynamic data from the Materials Project. You'll need an API key:

  1. Create an account at Materials Project
  2. Navigate to your API settings
  3. Set the environment variable:
export MP_API_KEY="your_api_key_here"

Or add it to your shell configuration file (.bashrc, .zshrc, etc.).

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
Test that everything is working:

from rxn_ca.utilities.get_entries import get_entries

# This should download phase data without errors
entries = get_entries(chem_sys="Mg-O", metastability_cutoff=0.03)
print(f"Found {len(entries)} phases")

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Once installation is complete, head to the Basic Usage guide to run your first simulation.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
This guide walks through running a complete solid-state reaction simulation using rxn-ca. We'll simulate the spinel reaction:

\[MgO + Al_{2}O_{3} \rightarrow MgAl_{2}O_{4}\]

docs.title: 1. Define the Synthesis Recipe
docs.text: The first step is to define a ReactionRecipe that specifies the reactants and heating profile.

docs.title: Specify Reactants
docs.text:
Express reactants as a dictionary of mole ratios:

reactants = {
    "MgO": 1,
    "Al2O3": 1,
}

docs.title: Create Heating Schedule
docs.text:
Heating profiles are defined using the HeatingSchedule class. Because reaction energies must be computed for every temperature, heating profiles consist of discrete steps rather than continuous sweeps.

from rxn_ca.core.recipe import ReactionRecipe
from rxn_ca.core.heating import HeatingSchedule, HeatingStep

heating_schedule = HeatingSchedule.build(
    HeatingStep.sweep(500, 1600, stage_length=1, temp_step_size=50),
    HeatingStep.hold(1600, stage_length=20)
)

recipe = ReactionRecipe(
    reactant_amounts=reactants,
    heating_schedule=heating_schedule
)

Stage Length

The stage_length parameter determines how long each temperature step is held. A reasonable starting point is integers between 1 and 10.

Save the recipe for later use:

recipe.to_file("spinel_recipe.json")

docs.title: 2. Collect Entries from Materials Project
docs.text: After defining the recipe, download phase entries from the Materials Project database. Each entry corresponds to a phase that might appear in the reaction pathway.

docs.title: Filtering Parameters
docs.text:
• Chemical System: The set of elements present in the reaction (precursors + atmosphere)
  • Metastability Cutoff: Maximum energy above the convex hull (in eV/atom). Phases with higher metastability are excluded.
  • Exclude Theoretical: Whether to exclude phases without ICSD observations
from rxn_ca.utilities.get_entries import get_entries

chem_sys = "Mg-Al-O"
metastability_cutoff = 0.03
exclude_theoretical = True

all_entries = get_entries(
    chem_sys=chem_sys,
    metastability_cutoff=metastability_cutoff,
    exclude_theoretical_phases=exclude_theoretical
)

print(f"Considering {len(all_entries)} phases")

docs.title: 3. Enumerate Reactions
docs.text:
Enumerate the reactions possible between the phases. This uses the reaction-network library.

from rxn_network.enumerators.utils import run_enumerators
from rxn_network.enumerators.basic import BasicEnumerator
from rxn_network.enumerators.minimize import MinimizeGibbsEnumerator

gibbs_enums = [MinimizeGibbsEnumerator(), BasicEnumerator()]
rxn_set = run_enumerators(gibbs_enums, all_entries)

docs.title: 4. Compute Temperature-Dependent Reaction Energies
docs.text:
The Gibbs formation energy of each phase depends on temperature. These values are computed using the SISSO descriptor which captures vibrational contributions to free energy.

all_temps = recipe.heating_schedule.all_temps
print(f"All temperatures required: {all_temps}")

temp_rxn_mapping = rxn_set.compute_at_temperatures(all_temps)

docs.title: 5. Score Reactions
docs.text:
Create a reaction library with scored reactions at each temperature:

from rxn_ca.utilities.get_scored_rxns import get_scored_rxns
from rxn_ca.phases import SolidPhaseSet
from monty.serialization import dumpfn

solid_phase_set = SolidPhaseSet.from_entry_set(all_entries)

reaction_library = get_scored_rxns(
    rxn_set,
    temps=all_temps,
    phase_set=solid_phase_set,
    rxns_at_temps=temp_rxn_mapping,
    parallel=True
)

dumpfn(reaction_library, "reaction_library.json")

docs.title: 6. Run the Simulation
docs.text:
With the recipe and reaction library files ready, run the simulation using the react CLI:

react spinel_recipe.json -l reaction_library.json -o output.json --compress

Compression

Always use --compress to reduce output file size to a manageable level.

docs.title: 7. Analyze Results
docs.text:
Visualize the evolution of phase amounts over the course of the reaction:

from rxn_ca.analysis.bulk_reaction_analyzer import BulkReactionAnalyzer
from rxn_ca.analysis.visualization.reaction_plotter import ReactionPlotter
from rxn_ca.analysis.visualization import PhaseTraceConfig

output_filename = "output_compressed.json"
analyzer = BulkReactionAnalyzer.from_result_doc_file(output_filename)

trace_config = PhaseTraceConfig(minimum_required_prevalence=0.00)
plotter = ReactionPlotter(analyzer, include_heating_trace=True)

plotter.plot_molar_phase_amounts()

The plot shows molar amounts of each phase over simulation time. The absolute numbers are not meaningful—only the relative amounts matter.

docs.title: Complete Script
docs.text:
Here's the complete workflow in a single script:

from rxn_ca.core.recipe import ReactionRecipe
from rxn_ca.core.heating import HeatingSchedule, HeatingStep
from rxn_ca.utilities.get_entries import get_entries
from rxn_ca.utilities.get_scored_rxns import get_scored_rxns
from rxn_ca.phases import SolidPhaseSet
from rxn_network.enumerators.utils import run_enumerators
from rxn_network.enumerators.basic import BasicEnumerator
from rxn_network.enumerators.minimize import MinimizeGibbsEnumerator
from monty.serialization import dumpfn

# 1. Define recipe
reactants = {"MgO": 1, "Al2O3": 1}
heating_schedule = HeatingSchedule.build(
    HeatingStep.sweep(500, 1600, stage_length=1, temp_step_size=50),
    HeatingStep.hold(1600, stage_length=20)
)
recipe = ReactionRecipe(reactant_amounts=reactants, heating_schedule=heating_schedule)

# 2. Get entries
all_entries = get_entries(
    chem_sys="Mg-Al-O",
    metastability_cutoff=0.03,
    exclude_theoretical_phases=True
)

# 3. Enumerate reactions
rxn_set = run_enumerators(
    [MinimizeGibbsEnumerator(), BasicEnumerator()],
    all_entries
)

# 4. Compute at temperatures
all_temps = recipe.heating_schedule.all_temps
temp_rxn_mapping = rxn_set.compute_at_temperatures(all_temps)

# 5. Score reactions
solid_phase_set = SolidPhaseSet.from_entry_set(all_entries)
reaction_library = get_scored_rxns(
    rxn_set,
    temps=all_temps,
    phase_set=solid_phase_set,
    rxns_at_temps=temp_rxn_mapping,
    parallel=True
)

# Save outputs
recipe.to_file("spinel_recipe.json")
dumpfn(reaction_library, "reaction_library.json")

# Run: react spinel_recipe.json -l reaction_library.json -o output.json --compress

docs.title: Using Jobflow for Parallel Simulations
docs.text:
While the Basic Usage guide works well for small-scale studies and debugging, most applications require running many simulations in parallel. This guide shows how to use Jobflow for high-throughput simulations on HPC infrastructure.

When to Use Jobflow

This approach is most useful when running hundreds or more simulations. For smaller numbers, the basic approach may be sufficient.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
We'll use the same MgAl₂O₄ spinel example. First, prepare the recipe and reaction library:

from rxn_ca.core.recipe import ReactionRecipe
from rxn_ca.core.heating import HeatingSchedule, HeatingStep
from rxn_ca.utilities.get_entries import get_entries
from rxn_ca.utilities.get_scored_rxns import get_scored_rxns
from rxn_ca.phases import SolidPhaseSet
from rxn_network.enumerators.utils import run_enumerators
from rxn_network.enumerators.basic import BasicEnumerator
from rxn_network.enumerators.minimize import MinimizeGibbsEnumerator

reactants = {"MgO": 1, "Al2O3": 1}

heating_schedule = HeatingSchedule.build(
    HeatingStep.sweep(500, 1600, stage_length=1, temp_step_size=50),
    HeatingStep.hold(1600, stage_length=20)
)

recipe = ReactionRecipe(
    reactant_amounts=reactants,
    heating_schedule=heating_schedule
)

# Get entries and enumerate reactions
all_entries = get_entries(
    chem_sys="Mg-Al-O",
    metastability_cutoff=0.03,
    exclude_theoretical_phases=True
)

rxn_set = run_enumerators(
    [MinimizeGibbsEnumerator(), BasicEnumerator()],
    all_entries
)

all_temps = recipe.heating_schedule.all_temps
temp_rxn_mapping = rxn_set.compute_at_temperatures(all_temps)

solid_phase_set = SolidPhaseSet.from_entry_set(all_entries)
reaction_library = get_scored_rxns(
    rxn_set,
    temps=all_temps,
    phase_set=solid_phase_set,
    rxns_at_temps=temp_rxn_mapping,
    parallel=True
)

docs.title: Creating Jobs with MultiRxnCAMaker
docs.text:
Jobflow allows you to create similar "jobs" and orchestrate complex workflows. The MultiRxnCAMaker handles parallel simulation jobs.

from rxn_ca.computing.jobs import MultiRxnCAMaker, MultiRxnType

docs.title: Simulation Types
docs.text:
There are three types of parallel simulations:

Type Description Use Case MULTI_RECIPE One reaction library, many recipes Testing different heating profiles MULTI_LIB One recipe, many reaction libraries Testing different scoring functions MULTI_LIB_AND_RECIPE Many recipes and many libraries Full parameter sweeps

docs.title: Creating Jobs
docs.text:
num_replications = 3

# Case 1: Multiple recipes, single library
maker = MultiRxnCAMaker(multi_rxn_type=MultiRxnType.MULTI_RECIPE)
job = maker.make(
    recipes=[recipe] * num_replications,
    reaction_libraries=reaction_library
)

# Case 2: Single recipe, multiple libraries
maker = MultiRxnCAMaker(multi_rxn_type=MultiRxnType.MULTI_LIB)
job = maker.make(
    recipes=recipe,
    reaction_libraries=[reaction_library] * num_replications
)

# Case 3: Multiple recipes and libraries (default)
maker = MultiRxnCAMaker(multi_rxn_type=MultiRxnType.MULTI_LIB_AND_RECIPE)
job = maker.make(
    recipes=[recipe] * num_replications,
    reaction_libraries=[reaction_library] * num_replications
)

docs.title: Running Jobs Locally
docs.text:
For testing or small runs:

from jobflow import run_locally

output = run_locally(job, create_folders=True)

Each simulation runs 3 replicates by default to better sample the system. With num_replications=3, this executes 9 total simulations.

CPU Usage

By default, each simulation uses 1 CPU. Run at least as many simulations as available cores for optimal performance. If you encounter memory errors, increase cpus_per_task to 2 or 4.

docs.title: Accessing Results
docs.text:
Results are stored in the job output:

job_output = output[job.uuid][1].output

docs.title: Viewing Plots
docs.text:
Analysis plots are stored in serialized format. Deserialize with Plotly:

from plotly.io import from_json

for i, serial_plot in enumerate(job_output.molar_fraction_plots):
    print(f"Plotting job {i}...")
    fig = from_json(serial_plot['figure_json'])
    fig.show()

docs.title: Accessing Raw Data
docs.text:
for i, serial_plot in enumerate(job_output.molar_fraction_plots):
    for phase, data in serial_plot['data'].items():
        print(f"{phase}: {data['y']}")

docs.title: Checking Convergence
docs.text:
print(job_output.have_simulations_converged)
# [False, False, False]  # Short simulations typically don't converge

If simulations haven't converged, consider increasing the simulation time. For detailed analysis, read the full result documents from job_output.run_dir.

docs.title: Running on HPC with FireWorks
docs.text:
For large-scale runs, use FireWorks to manage workflows:

from jobflow.manager.fireworks import flow_to_workflow
from fireworks.core.launchpad import LaunchPad

lp = LaunchPad.from_file("fw_config.yaml")
wf = flow_to_workflow(job)
lp.add_wf(wf)

Then on your HPC cluster:

# Single workflow
qlaunch singleshot -f <fw_id>
# or
rlaunch singleshot -f <fw_id>

# Many workflows in parallel
qlaunch rapidfire --nlaunches <num_launches>

Refer to the Jobflow documentation and FireWorks documentation for detailed setup instructions.
